does: This paper investigates the claimed stability improvements of archetypal sparse autoencoders (SAEs) by systematically ablating their design choices. The authors demonstrate that the apparent stability of archetypal SAEs reported in prior work results primarily from shared initialization across training runs rather than from genuine convergence toward consistent features. Through careful empirical analysis, they show that when SAEs are trained with different random seeds from standard initialization, the archetypal variants fail to produce more stable dictionaries than baseline SAE approaches. The paper includes quantitative comparisons measuring feature agreement across independently trained models and identifies how specific architectural and training choices influence the measured stability metrics.
connects: This work builds on recent efforts to make sparse autoencoders more interpretable and reliable tools for mechanistic interpretability, extending prior analysis of SAE instability documented in the dictionary learning literature. It directly engages with archetypal SAEs proposed by Fel et al. (2025) as a method for improving feature stability and reducing polysemanticity in neural network activations. The paper contributes to growing interest in using SAE features as stable units for understanding language models, following alongside work in mechanistic interpretability that increasingly relies on feature stability as evidence for interpretability. It also connects to broader research on dictionary learning initialization schemes and their effects on learned representations across multiple domains.
enables: This work clarifies crucial conceptual distinctions for future mechanistic interpretability research, particularly the difference between stability (agreement between independently trained models) and stabilization (convergence from different initializations). These clarifications enable more rigorous evaluation of proposed SAE improvements and strengthen standards for claiming that SAE features represent reusable units of analysis. By identifying initialization as a key confound, the paper points toward future work developing truly initialization-invariant dictionary learning methods and more robust metrics for assessing feature reliability. This foundation supports better methodology for using SAE features in interpretability studies, where distinguishing genuine algorithmic structure from training artifacts is essential for reliable neural network analysis.
